target.name: dasyscyphin A
target.motivation: To demonstrate the utility of a quaternary carbon stereocenter epimerization protocol to override intrinsic stereochemical bias of polyolefin cyclization reactions and access a stereochemically rich tricyclic framework with an unconventional trans-anti-cis stereochemical array and a densely functionalized D-ring.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Au-catalyzed Rautenstrauch rearrangement for dasyscyphin B

success_criteria.free_text: Stereoselective synthesis of the tricyclic 6/6/5 terpenoid architecture, featuring an unconventional trans-anti-cis stereochemical array and a densely functionalized D-ring.

key_disconnections.named_strategy: annulative sequence featuring aldol addition and reductive radical cyclization
key_disconnections.reasoning: To construct the highly functionalized D-ring.
To access the trans-anti-cis substructure from an all-trans polycyclic scaffold, overriding the intrinsic stereochemical bias of polyolefin cyclization.
To construct the core tricyclic architecture from a linear polyene precursor.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The synthesis of dasyscyphin A was envisioned to proceed from farnesol, building up the linear polyene, followed by a polyolefin cyclization to form the all-trans tricyclic core. A key quaternary carbon epimerization step would then reconfigure the stereochemistry to the desired trans-anti-cis motif. Finally, the highly functionalized D-ring would be constructed via an annulative sequence involving aldol addition and reductive radical cyclization.

thought.background: Farnesol (18) is a commercially available linear polyene.
thought.gap: Need to homologate the allylic alcohol to a γ,δ-unsaturated nitrile.
thought.decision: To achieve a direct two-carbon homologation, chose Zaragoza’s method using (cyanomethyl)tributylphosphonium iodide, expecting excellent yield and avoiding unstable/pyrophoric reagents.

outcome.observation: Farnesol (18) was converted to farnesyl acetonitrile (19) in 70% yield.

evidence.text_span: our synthesis of dasyscyphin A initiated with conversion of farnesol (18) to farnesyl acetonitrile (19) employing an analogous Zaragoza homologation reaction (Scheme 3).

thought.background: Farnesyl acetonitrile (19) has a terminal olefin.
thought.gap: Need to introduce an enantioenriched epoxide at the terminal olefin.
thought.decision: To form the enantioenriched epoxide, chose sequential asymmetric dihydroxylation using Os-catalyzed conditions with Corey-Noe-Lin ligand, followed by mesylate formation and epoxidation, expecting excellent yield and selectivity.

outcome.observation: Sequential asymmetric dihydroxylation of the terminal olefin and formation of the enantioenriched epoxide generated cyclization precursor 8 in 41% yield and 97% ee.

evidence.text_span: Sequential asymmetric dihydroxylation of the terminal olefin and formation of the enantioenriched epoxide generated cyclization precursor 8 in excellent yield and selectivity (97% ee).

thought.background: Epoxynitrile 8 is a polyolefin cyclization precursor.
thought.gap: Need to form the tricyclic core with an all-trans configuration.
thought.decision: To construct the polycyclic framework, chose Ti-catalyzed polyolefin cyclization, expecting the all-trans configuration.

outcome.observation: Ti-catalyzed polyolefin cyclization yielded alcohol 20 with the all-trans configuration in 49% yield.

evidence.text_span: Ti-catalyzed polyolefin cyclization and tert-butyldimethylsilyl (TBS) protection of the ensuing secondary alcohol provided 7 with the all-trans configuration.

thought.gap: Need to protect the secondary alcohol.
thought.decision: To protect the secondary alcohol, chose TBS protection, expecting to form 7.

outcome.observation: TBS protection of the secondary alcohol provided 7 in 81% yield.

evidence.text_span: Ti-catalyzed polyolefin cyclization and tert-butyldimethylsilyl (TBS) protection of the ensuing secondary alcohol provided 7 with the all-trans configuration.

thought.background: Ketone 7 has the all-trans configuration at C8.
thought.gap: Need to adjust the stereochemistry at C8 to the desired trans-anti-cis configuration.
thought.decision: To revise the configuration of the D-ring, chose the photochemical epimerization protocol, expecting excellent yield and diastereoselectivity.

outcome.observation: The stereochemistry at C8 was smoothly adjusted with the epimerization protocol, and the tricyclic core (6) of dasyscyphin A was accessed in 69% yield and >20:1 dr.

evidence.text_span: We were able to smoothly adjust the stereochemistry at C8 with our epimerization protocol, and the tricyclic core (6) of dasyscyphin A was accessed in 69% yield and >20:1 dr.

thought.background: Ketone 6 is the tricyclic core of dasyscyphin A.
thought.gap: Need to construct the highly functionalized D-ring.
thought.decision: To construct the D-ring, chose a Ti-mediated diastereoselective aldol addition with 4-pentenal, expecting to generate alcohol 22.

outcome.observation: A Ti-mediated diastereoselective aldol addition generated alcohol 22 in 75% yield.

evidence.text_span: A Ti-mediated diastereoselective aldol addition generated alcohol 22, followed by conversion to the corresponding silyl ether.

thought.background: Alcohol 22 has a secondary alcohol and a pendant alkene.
thought.gap: Need to protect the secondary alcohol and cleave the alkene to an aldehyde.
thought.decision: To protect the alcohol and form the aldehyde, chose TESOTf/Et3N for silyl ether formation, followed by ozonolysis and reductive workup, expecting to produce aldehyde 23.

outcome.observation: Conversion to the corresponding silyl ether and ozonolysis of the pendant alkene produced aldehyde 23 in 91% yield.

evidence.text_span: A Ti-mediated diastereoselective aldol addition generated alcohol 22, followed by conversion to the corresponding silyl ether. Ozonolysis of the pendant alkene produced aldehyde 23 and set the stage for reductive cyclization.

thought.background: Aldehyde 23 is ready for reductive cyclization.
thought.gap: Need to perform a SmI2-mediated ketyl radical cyclization.
thought.decision: To achieve reductive cyclization, chose SmI2-mediated ketyl radical cyclization, expecting intramolecular cyclization.

outcome.observation: Preliminary investigations revealed that the SmI2-mediated ketyl radical cyclization was more difficult than anticipated. This was likely due to the sterically encumbered ketone and predominantly led to aldehyde reduction and dimerization. Deuterium-labeling experiments indicated that the reduced byproduct arises from HAT with THF rather than proton transfer.

evidence.text_span: Our preliminary investigations revealed that the SmI2-mediated ketyl radical cyclization was more difficult than anticipated. This was likely due to the sterically encumbered ketone and predominantly led to aldehyde reduction and dimerization. Optimization revealed that using the PhMe as solvent suppresses the competing reduction of aldehyde by minimizing solvent-mediated hydrogen atom transfer. Consistent with this interpretation, deuterium-labeling experiments indicated that the reduced byproduct arises from HAT with THF rather than proton transfer (see the Supporting Information for details).

thought.background: SmI2-mediated ketyl radical cyclization in THF led to aldehyde reduction and dimerization.
thought.gap: Need to suppress competing aldehyde reduction and promote productive ketyl radical formation and intramolecular cyclization.
thought.decision: To improve the cyclization, chose PhMe as solvent to minimize solvent-mediated hydrogen atom transfer, and LiBr as an additive to increase the reducing ability of SmI2, expecting productive ketyl radical formation and intramolecular cyclization.

outcome.observation: Optimization revealed that using PhMe as solvent suppresses the competing reduction of aldehyde, and LiBr as an additive increased the reducing ability of SmI2, promoting productive ketyl radical formation and intramolecular cyclization, yielding 24 in 47% yield.

evidence.text_span: Optimization revealed that using the PhMe as solvent suppresses the competing reduction of aldehyde by minimizing solvent-mediated hydrogen atom transfer. Consistent with this interpretation, deuterium-labeling experiments indicated that the reduced byproduct arises from HAT with THF rather than proton transfer (see the Supporting Information for details). Further examination of the cyclization conditions showed that LiBr as an additive increased the reducing ability of SmI2, thus promoting productive ketyl radical formation from the sterically hindered ketone and intramolecular cyclization, rather than intermolecular aldehyde dimerization. A Parikh−Doering oxidation of the secondary alcohol provided 24 in 92% yield.

thought.gap: Need to oxidize the secondary alcohol.
thought.decision: To oxidize the secondary alcohol, chose Parikh−Doering oxidation, expecting high yield.

outcome.observation: A Parikh−Doering oxidation of the secondary alcohol provided 24 in 92% yield.

evidence.text_span: A Parikh−Doering oxidation of the secondary alcohol provided 24 in 92% yield.

thought.background: Ketone 24 needs a methyl group introduced at C15.
thought.gap: Need to introduce a methyl group at C15 with the correct configuration.
thought.decision: To introduce the methyl group, chose alkylation of the corresponding enolate intermediate with iodomethane, expecting to form the methylated product.

outcome.observation: Attempts to directly methylate 24 through alkylation of the corresponding enolate intermediate with iodomethane led to the incorrect configuration at C15 along with dimethylation.

evidence.text_span: Attempts to directly methylate 24 through alkylation of the corresponding enolate intermediate with iodomethane led to the incorrect configuration at C15 along with the dimethylation.

thought.background: Direct methylation of 24 led to incorrect stereochemistry and dimethylation.
thought.gap: Need to introduce the methyl group at C15 with the correct configuration and avoid dimethylation.
thought.decision: To circumvent the issues, chose a sequential methylenation and in situ Pd/C hydrogenation, expecting to introduce the methyl group selectively.

outcome.observation: A sequential methylenation and in situ Pd/C hydrogenation was used to introduce the methyl group, yielding 25 in 92% yield (over two steps with previous oxidation).

evidence.text_span: To circumvent this, a sequential methylenation and in situ Pd/C hydrogenation was used to introduce the methyl group. Removal of the silyl protecting groups completed the first total synthesis of dasyscyphin A (2) in 13 steps.

thought.gap: Need to remove the silyl protecting groups to complete the synthesis.
thought.decision: To remove the silyl protecting groups, chose HF-Py, expecting to unveil dasyscyphin A (2).

outcome.observation: Removal of the silyl protecting groups completed the first total synthesis of dasyscyphin A (2) in 84% yield.

evidence.text_span: Removal of the silyl protecting groups completed the first total synthesis of dasyscyphin A (2) in 13 steps.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield not explicitly stated, but individual step yields are provided.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: The synthesis was completed in 13 steps.

Total Steps.interpretation: The synthesis was completed in 13 steps.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Two failed attempts were reported during the synthesis.

validation.comparison_to_prior: Dasyscyphin A has not been previously synthesized. Dasyscyphin B was synthesized by Sarlah and co-workers using a Au-catalyzed Rautenstrauch rearrangement, demonstrating complementary access to the tricyclic architecture through catalyst-controlled cyclization strategies.
validation.significance: This work demonstrates a concise strategy for the rapid assembly of the core framework of dasyscyphin A through sequential polyolefin cyclization and quaternary carbon center epimerization. By overriding the inherent stereochemical bias of polyolefin cyclization reactions, this approach enables access to polycyclic architectures bearing unconventional stereochemical patterns that would traditionally require multistep synthetic sequences. This introduces a retrosynthetic paradigm in which bond construction and stereochemical installation are strategically decoupled.

source.title: Overriding Stereochemical Outcomes in Cyclase Phase Total Synthesis: Enantioselective Synthesis of Habiterpenol and Dasyscyphin A.
source.authors: Wu L, Nguyen LH, Yan L, Yin H, Mizuno N, Schuppe AW.
